title: One-Class Learning: Training Only on Normal
concept: One-class learning trains a model exclusively on 'normal' examples, teaching it to recognize the boundary of normality so tightly that anything falling outside that boundary — regardless of how it's abnormal — gets flagged.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does anomaly detection handle novel, previously unseen defect types better than a fixed classifier?

options.text: It flags anything deviating from learned normality, without needing prior labeled examples of that specific defect
It requires labeling every possible defect type in advance
It cannot process any images at all

exercises.explain: Yes! Anomaly detection generalizes to novel deviations without needing prior labels.

cards.title: Training with only the good examples
cards.body: Imagine training a security guard to spot suspicious behavior by showing them ONLY hours of footage of completely normal, everyday activity — never a single example of an actual crime. Surprisingly, this is exactly how one-class anomaly models are trained.

cards.title: One-Class Learning
cards.body: One-class learning trains a model using ONLY examples of the normal class, teaching it to build a tight boundary around what 'normal' looks like in feature space. At inference, anything falling OUTSIDE this boundary — no matter how it differs — gets flagged as anomalous.

cards.title: Building a fence around the herd
cards.body: Imagine building a fence tightly around a herd of sheep in a field, using only the sheep's actual positions to decide where the fence goes. Anything found OUTSIDE that fence later — a wolf, a lost dog, a stray cow — gets flagged, even though you never specifically trained the fence to recognize wolves, dogs, or cows.

cards.title: Metal part inspection
cards.body: A model is trained only on embeddings of thousands of correctly manufactured metal parts, learning the tight cluster region these normal parts occupy in feature space. A part with a novel warp, crack, or discoloration lands OUTSIDE this learned normal region and gets flagged, even without ever seeing that exact defect before.

cards.title: The model doesn't know WHY something is anomalous
cards.body: It's a myth that a one-class model understands the specific TYPE of anomaly it detects. It only knows the anomaly falls outside the learned normal boundary — determining the actual root cause (crack vs. scratch vs. contamination) usually requires a separate follow-up analysis step, often by a human inspector.

left.text:
Requires labeled defect examples
Struggles with unseen defect types
Standard supervised classification

right.text:
Requires ONLY normal examples
Flags anything outside normal boundary
Handles unseen defect types naturally

exercises.prompt: What data does one-class learning require during training?

options.text: Only examples of the normal class
An equal number of normal and defective examples
Only examples labeled with specific defect types

exercises.explain: Correct! One-class learning trains exclusively on normal examples.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A one-class anomaly model can tell you the specific TYPE of defect it detected (e.g. 'crack' vs. 'scratch').
exercises.explain: False. It only knows something falls outside the learned normal boundary; identifying the specific defect type usually requires further analysis.

exercises.sentence: One-class learning builds a tight ___ around what normal examples look like in feature space.
exercises.explain: The learned boundary defines the region of 'normal'; anything outside it is flagged.

exercises.prompt: Why can a one-class model flag a never-before-seen defect type, like a novel metal warp pattern it wasn't specifically trained on?

options.text: It only checks whether the input falls outside the learned normal boundary, regardless of the specific way it differs
It secretly memorized every possible defect type during training
It randomly guesses whether an input is defective

exercises.explain: Correct! The boundary-based approach generalizes to any deviation, known or novel.

exercises.prompt: A quality control engineer asks a one-class anomaly detection system 'why' it flagged a specific part as defective, hoping for a specific root-cause explanation. What should they expect?

options.text: The model can confirm the part falls outside the learned normal boundary, but usually cannot explain the specific root cause without additional analysis
The model will automatically and precisely name the exact defect type with full certainty
The model cannot flag any part it hasn't seen a labeled example of before
One-class models never produce any output explanation of any kind

exercises.explain: Exactly! Boundary-based flagging doesn't inherently explain the root cause. Next: a popular technique for building this normal boundary — reconstruction-based detection.
